subject: Most engineers are investing in the wrong skills right now
body:
Inside: a boost to help you build the right ones

Денис,

Most engineers I talk to can list the skills they want to add. Very few have thought seriously about which ones they should drop.

That's the problem with a fast-moving industry. Your stack accumulates. You keep carrying knowledge that mattered three years ago but doesn't compound anymore. And the longer you carry it, the less room you have for the things that actually move your career forward.

So here's what I've been telling people lately, and what we'll dive into with today's newsletter: before you add anything new, audit what you're already carrying.

A quick note before we get started: today kicks off our Spring Flash Sale, where you can get up to 50% off (or more) (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ2K3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3p_W5FKKy61HsPjzW2q1dGZ3kKd4HW5slw7R6syY9PW5X0stb8Y_M4TW3cg5xz25nJgpN48jbxBkMFSsW8HJJR97Y_cRCW40Nfbf2gSBHrW863--P4ShqZzW45Rk5b6-pbs1W93KQj34F08zHW7qp9Cr2249CyW3nrF4f8HX5JkW96BmJ38X1q2lW98QF6847fKKqN3F3t6yxm5s9W1BRjvq8r-CkjN2hL8BK_5wQdW5433764S2PlCW2ndjnL7NkDq-W7JvwWz7WXwwTVZL-Fb4SB_4XW51Jx1m1SM8YMW6nTypn7wVRXvW5VTfZp6q03S5N6684s5HD6MTW8QkCFs5Fd81_MxnR0SjSDXbf8GBgRM04 ) an Educative unlimited subscription. If there's a skill you've been meaning to sharpen, this is the time.

See your offer
(https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ2K3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3p_W5FKKy61HsPjzW2q1dGZ3kKd4HW5slw7R6syY9PW5X0stb8Y_M4TW3cg5xz25nJgpN48jbxBkMFSsW8HJJR97Y_cRCW40Nfbf2gSBHrW863--P4ShqZzW45Rk5b6-pbs1W93KQj34F08zHW7qp9Cr2249CyW3nrF4f8HX5JkW96BmJ38X1q2lW98QF6847fKKqN3F3t6yxm5s9W1BRjvq8r-CkjN2hL8BK_5wQdW5433764S2PlCW2ndjnL7NkDq-W7JvwWz7WXwwTVZL-Fb4SB_4XW51Jx1m1SM8YMW6nTypn7wVRXvW5VTfZp6q03S5N6684s5HD6MTW8QkCFs5Fd81_MxnR0SjSDXbf8GBgRM04 )

Two questions worth asking right now

Here's a quick exercise worth doing this month. Look at the technical skills you spend the most time on, and ask two questions:

1. Is this skill appreciating or depreciating?

Some skills compound over time. System design, architectural reasoning, the ability to evaluate tradeoffs — these get more valuable as systems get more complex and as AI handles more of the implementation layer. Other skills are depreciating. If AI can do it faster and more reliably than you, the market value of doing it manually is falling.

That doesn't mean you stop understanding the fundamentals. It means you stop leading with them.

2. Am I learning this, or just maintaining it?

There's a difference between actively building a new capability and keeping an old one warm out of habit. Maintenance feels productive. But if a skill stopped teaching you something new two years ago, the time you spend on it is borrowed from something with a higher ceiling.

Matthew Baldwin (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ2q3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3nLW6Cf34327Y72NN774krXhKJTjW54l_ww7v6mp8W3vtcl63LqRpkN93pJk4_xmZWW7k-y4Q2zQW3kW64jZg25mD4CjW2F4bBR5cYMF0VVmFDV22pPD1VyW4Cx75N4vqW1rtThb6hggJsW6zl1_V8rmGhDV6x7X84RrzbjW2CrmTN68TKqJVj6wjn4R8HwsW2CDvRq5knjk5V9nFCC3K-mzNW22KqJJ5JWpzCW7j6hFc92Vbx2V7W5xt1Y7N4XW6Q0vKr5yvHhTW3-6pgG8rzvmVW8Fsh205ttK58W6K4g8M8DDKMkW8_f1KS98NRnfW96HYZ_2bFSP5f1ktgkP04 )

Principal Engineer @ AWS

"Educative’s hands-on quizzes and real-world case studies consistently bridge theory to practice, helping me stay sharp and lead high-scale engineering efforts."

Where I'd reinvest

If I were doing my own spring cleaning, I'd be doubling down on three areas:

1. Treating AI as a disciplined engineering practice, not a shortcut.

There's a version of AI-assisted development where you paste a prompt, get code back, and ship it. That works until it doesn't.

The engineers I respect most are using AI differently: they scope tasks clearly before prompting, they review output the way they'd review code from a new hire, and they treat every suggestion as untested until proven otherwise.

The real skill isn't getting AI to generate code. It's knowing when the code it generates is subtly wrong. Like when it handles the happy path but misses edge cases, when it looks correct but carries insecure defaults, when it works in isolation but breaks under real load.

I think of it this way: AI is a junior teammate with broad knowledge but zero accountability. Your job is to be the senior engineer in the room, even if you're early in your career. That means learning to read code critically, bound its blast radius before running it, and prove correctness through tests rather than assumption.

If you're looking to build this muscle, our Agentic System Design (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ303prCCW95jsWP6lZ3pyW1lxRlg618g48W81_jMx7-GgT9W13bG293V-p8SN5J4gs3kHWzFW3wDzPN1cPTT3W1wdTqg1G6Mn0N53TtJDLtq6fW78cxxk4Fmv9GW6P-yqC4tTKVPVG5V6_2SrgzvVbFBJB64rRC9W8K2-vB4KW5QNW8SyvWv3SPTFGV89vT36yKW55W23Sc8Y3w0JDFVCKcdG8LB_RtW1s_bgf6sksPlW2-sZJJ3HbqGdW7Q9-_n6nZKH3W1n0wpf8tctjRW7lPPVs32PGDdW3wXvvs6s2lJlW1NQYxp2G_MQ6W1HkGks5YM70JW46s0-58MkZ4-W6tqmf654H8rLW40bDLy48tljZW6sfLG28j293fN5FKZFhfXLBtF87h-XGVslDf1T1G-404 ) course is the best place to start. It covers the architectures and decision-making patterns behind AI-powered systems, which is increasingly where the real engineering work lives.

2. System design and architectural thinking.

As code generation gets cheaper, the ability to decide what to build and how systems should fit together is where the leverage sits. This has been true for a while, but it's accelerating. When AI can scaffold a feature in minutes, the bottleneck shifts to the person who understands why the system is structured a certain way, what breaks at scale, and which tradeoffs are acceptable for this particular context.

No universal correct architecture exists. Twitter's write-heavy pattern looks nothing like Facebook's complex graph queries, which looks nothing like Uber's geospatial routing. The judgment to match a design to its constraints is something AI can't do for you. It's also one of the most durable skills you can build.

If system design is a gap you've been meaning to close, Grokking System Design Interview (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ0Y5m_5PW50kH_H6lZ3l8W3rY9q1348m5GW55WrTp1dtrS7W7_j8Q5108vkZW3NNgGn56C6dpW5qpDln5zZV6_W1KS-kX8BsvMBW1Rqctv7SrLx_W5yMHtZ4LXlFrW3t7nfP5f8HHRW1m6vvq1jYR6qW17jhGj2Nhg0XW7rkgm763hg3RVx1l5c6ks_ZJW97ss9F1hTP3dW4CVC8S2xfF_PW2tX1Qp70ZXSGW8tCjlw3JHZSMW4h6nq_8VJ-F3W8Crhvn23dzqWW8TtxtH57LsFdN1-mzZrG63zJW3VCKN51tH0yHW2-GWRQ3wW1r8W6QPyBb5W1v2FW3TPzQJ8ct-xHW9d6Xs14GBLPLVJPc1V3yX-jpF7B7-gdg9rpW5Jh3N_4njzYHW7Z_4Hb7-9qdhW1WnlY44KM-VbW3MFb7g5_98RGf7LVXG204 ) is the resource I'd point you toward.

3. Pattern recognition over problem volume.

This applies especially if you're actively interviewing or expect to be soon. I see too many engineers grinding through hundreds of problems without building the underlying pattern recognition that actually transfers. Recognition doesn't come from random practice. It comes from studying a few patterns deeply enough that you can spot them under time pressure: two pointers, sliding window, DFS vs. BFS, binary search on monotonic conditions, dynamic programming with clearly defined state.

The engineers who do well in compressed hiring timelines aren't the ones who solved the most problems. They're the ones who pause, frame the problem clearly, and explain their approach (even if they don't finish). Clarity is a differentiator when the market is crowded.

Grokking the Coding Interview Patterns (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ1d5m_5PW5BWr2F6lZ3mPW6y7Dy64c7Xb2W1wNcrB9kyfTMW8vKxBr9dpXzsW3ybCLx93XmP5W2KZSB-80vbL4W1X5Bj95bFq-kN5b2CmMmvcDLN8MP3-9flg25W1BDF909d2YjHW72kL3g4wvjPCW813wh99lhtmjW4-tHdg8K-C4hW16W90J57gKCsW7XHwCW4z4BtmW9bsdsh1VS1R7N25wPRSR3NfFW3_bQ2y81PZM8W7Jp-Yz6D7D6zW5LM_872bJcDyV309vf8MnW83W4xPSk_8c0MCyN3byGS1pFPlkW85NwvH910pyKW5PjYyc4n3B-9W63QxpQ7Ssl3KW1VxPZf4_WT0lW4fN8jZ19tdtxW393vbM5BCRKqW9g0NLX6s5C1hW6tNwSx5SqqfLW4jvYfQ4Nvb4HW8GBCbM8xf4gFW8y3rN55d1nZzW3GNb-F2M07Qwf3kGvRs04 )  was built for exactly this — pattern-first prep that transfers across problems rather than memorizing individual solutions.

Nishal Pattan (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ2q3prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3pBN3ctbmyN8gPhW8rwglX7x-2hGW2N6VSR5dKy8CW6BDGbY7v3C_3W8T6Q9S6xnWXtW7vdpTF141kkTW71qhzG7zJZrdW9dtKJ35C0yV5W8t4Wqm5yjNYnW1ZW1hS1hzZbjW7fgbHp4sJwx5W2z6kN93FZDcZW164F6g4kxYNDW18vJ4f72RmvRVGqjM_5ZQ6LDW7PR6CP4PWDlHW8KZcP82B-WfYW1tsHF62-f-BgVL66_d1rXNWmW2c845r1_LmmkW7WWgKJ1P8XymW3mrmgn4Kxh7yW7yYBc_5GJlDzW38mdxk7JnpWSW6K641x1Yb2LQN5YqN0yflkJjf2nQTPH04 )

Backend Software Engineer @ Microsoft

"Educative.io has been a key partner in my growth. These hands-on, structured paths have accelerated my interview prep for roles at Meta, Uber, and Oracle, while deepening my confidence."

The real spring cleaning

The engineers who feel stuck right now often aren't missing knowledge. They're carrying too much of the wrong kind, and haven't made room for what the market actually rewards today.

Right now seems like a reasonable time to fix that.

Get 50% off (or more)
(https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VWD2yl49xd92W77Tt8_5_1ltsW1c61D75L_LSPN7PgQ2K3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3p_W5FKKy61HsPjzW2q1dGZ3kKd4HW5slw7R6syY9PW5X0stb8Y_M4TW3cg5xz25nJgpN48jbxBkMFSsW8HJJR97Y_cRCW40Nfbf2gSBHrW863--P4ShqZzW45Rk5b6-pbs1W93KQj34F08zHW7qp9Cr2249CyW3nrF4f8HX5JkW96BmJ38X1q2lW98QF6847fKKqN3F3t6yxm5s9W1BRjvq8r-CkjN2hL8BK_5wQdW5433764S2PlCW2ndjnL7NkDq-W7JvwWz7WXwwTVZL-Fb4SB_4XW51Jx1m1SM8YMW6nTypn7wVRXvW5VTfZp6q03S5N6684s5HD6MTW8QkCFs5Fd81_MxnR0SjSDXbf8GBgRM04 )

Until next time,

- Fahim
